content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Consolidation with mitigation (closure as default, compensatory measures)'. Any rural school consolidation policy should be sequenced around the 2027–2030 demographic decline window using per-settlement differentiated criteria and mandatory transport lead times, rather than pursued as a blanket teacher-shortage rationalization, since evidence shows small schools are not the shortage's primary driver.
opportunities: Enables investment concentration in fewer, better-equipped consolidated sites, following the Portuguese centros escolares pattern of matched receiving-site investment.
Statutory transport guarantees and targeted support provide a formal, legally enforceable floor of protection for displaced pupils, an improvement over ad hoc arrangements.
strengths: Teacher shortages in Hungary are structurally concentrated in rural and disadvantaged schools, with 2–3 times higher rates of unqualified teachers than in non-disadvantaged schools, and half of all teachers nationally are now 50 or older.

Small rural schools are not the cause of the national teacher shortage; researchers note the shortage has grown fastest in Budapest and large cities, and small schools represent barely 1% of the whole system.
threats: Compensatory measures (transport guarantees, TEIP-style support) require sustained multi-year funding; without a specified binding funding-commitment mechanism (e.g., ring-fenced budget line, multi-year statutory allocation), these measures risk being cut in future budget cycles once the initial closure has already occurred and political attention has moved on.

Closure decisions, even with consultation, may trigger community mobilization (as in the 2007 Erdőkürt 'Human Chain for Rural Schools' precedent), and limited judicial review (legality-only, not policy-merit) means affected communities have few formal channels to contest closure substance.
weaknesses: It is unknown whether the government's current (2026) education administration has revived, abandoned, or redesigned the 2024 leaked consolidation plan; no recent official confirmation was found.

The precise current count of villages under 2,000 population with an operating primary school, and how many meet the EU's <10 per class or <100–120 total threshold, is not established (only a dated 2008 estimate of ~109–300 small schools exists).
